lead: Getting your piano safely into its new home is only half the story. What you do in the days and weeks afterwards decides whether it settles happily or starts to misbehave. A piano is a living thing of wood, felt and steel, and it needs a little patience to adjust to a new room. Having moved and resettled countless instruments across Suffolk, here is our honest guidance on caring for your piano after a move.

sections.body:
The single most useful thing you can do for a piano after a move is also the easiest: leave it alone for a while. A piano has just been wrapped, lifted, wheeled, driven and carried into an unfamiliar room, and it needs time to acclimatise to its new surroundings before it is fussed over. Resist the urge to start playing it hard straight away or to call the tuner the moment it is in place. Give it space to settle into the temperature and humidity of its new home.

The reason for this patience is that a piano responds to its environment. The wood and felt inside react to the warmth and the moisture in the air, and after a move the instrument needs a couple of weeks, ideally a little longer, to reach a stable state in the new room. Tune it or judge it too soon and you will be reacting to a piano that is still adjusting, which is wasted effort. A short period of patience now saves frustration later.

This settling period is also a good moment to make sure the piano is in the right place from the start, which we come to shortly, so that it can acclimatise where it is going to stay rather than being shuffled around the room afterwards. In our experience, owners who give their piano this calm settling-in time are far happier with how it plays and sounds once it has properly found its feet in the new home.

sections.h: Finding the Right Spot in the Room
sections.body:
Where you put a piano matters a great deal to how well it survives and sounds, so it is worth getting the position right while it is settling. The main thing to avoid is anywhere that exposes the instrument to heat, damp or sudden changes in either. Keep it away from radiators, fireplaces and underfloor heating, which dry the wood and can crack the soundboard, and away from external doors and windows where draughts and damp creep in. A spot against an internal wall is usually kindest.

Direct sunlight is another thing to guard against. Strong sun will fade and damage the finish over time and heat the case unevenly, so a position out of the glare, or with curtains or blinds to soften it, is far better for the instrument. Try, too, to place the piano somewhere the room temperature stays reasonably steady, rather than in a hall or conservatory that swings from cold to hot and back across the day and the seasons.

If we are placing the piano for you as part of the move, do tell us where you are thinking of putting it, and we are happy to advise on whether it is a good spot. Getting the position right at the point of delivery means the piano can acclimatise where it will stay, and you avoid the awkward, risky business of trying to shift a heavy instrument across the room later once you realise the first choice was too close to the radiator.

sections.h: When and How to Tune After a Move
sections.body:
Almost every piano will need tuning after a move, and understanding why helps you time it well. The combination of the physical journey, the jolts and vibrations of travel, and above all the change of environment, will nudge a piano out of tune to some degree, even when it has been handled perfectly. This is entirely normal and nothing to worry about; it is simply what happens when a finely strung instrument moves from one room and climate to another.

The key is not to tune too early. As covered already, the piano needs to settle into the temperature and humidity of its new home first, so the usual advice is to wait at least a couple of weeks, and often a few weeks more, before having it tuned. Tune it the day it arrives and it will likely drift again as it acclimatises, wasting the effort. Let it stabilise, then book a tuner, and mention to them that the instrument has recently been moved so they know what to expect.

A move can be a good prompt to build a relationship with a reliable local piano tuner if you do not already have one, since regular tuning keeps any piano in good health. After the post-move tuning has settled it back into tune, returning to a sensible regular schedule will keep it sounding its best. Do not be alarmed if it needs a touch more attention than usual in the first few months after a move; that is the instrument finding its balance in a new place.

sections.h: Managing Climate in the New Home
sections.body:
Beyond finding the right spot, it pays to think about the general climate of the room your piano lives in, because steady conditions are what keep an instrument healthy over time. Pianos are happiest with a reasonably stable temperature and a moderate, consistent level of humidity. Wild swings, a room that is freezing overnight and baking by afternoon, or air that is very dry in winter and damp in summer, are harder on a piano than a steady, ordinary household environment.

If your new home is noticeably drier or damper than the old one, the piano may take a little while to adjust, and you might notice small changes in how it feels or sounds during that time. Keeping heating reasonably even, avoiding placing the piano right next to sources of heat or damp, and not letting the room get extreme in either direction all help. In some homes, owners choose to manage humidity more actively, and a piano tuner can advise on what suits your particular instrument and room if you have concerns.

The aim is simply to give the piano a calm, consistent place to live. We've seen instruments thrive for decades in perfectly ordinary rooms that happen to stay steady, and others struggle in fancier settings that swing between extremes. After a move, paying a little attention to the climate of the new room is one of the kindest things you can do for the instrument, and it sets it up to settle well and stay in good condition for years to come.

Once the piano has settled and been tuned, a little everyday care keeps it looking and playing its best, and a move is a natural moment to start good habits. For the case, gentle dusting with a soft, dry or very slightly damp cloth is usually all that is needed; harsh cleaners and sprays can harm the finish, so it is best to keep things simple. Always dust gently and follow the grain, and wipe up any spills or marks promptly before they have a chance to settle into the surface.

The keys deserve care too. A soft cloth, lightly dampened if necessary, keeps them clean, and it is wise to avoid soaking them or letting moisture run down between them. Keeping the lid closed when the piano is not in use protects the keys from dust and from sunlight, and it is a small habit that makes a real difference over the years. Discourage drinks and small items from being left on top of the piano, as a spill or a scratch is easily avoided and not always easily undone.

It is also worth giving the instrument a gentle once-over after the move to check the case for any marks and to make sure everything feels and sounds as it should once it has settled. If anything seems off, raise it; a piano that has been moved by specialists and is properly covered gives you reassurance, and a tuner can address any small adjustments the instrument needs as it beds into its new home. Beyond that, simply playing it regularly is one of the best things for a piano.

sections.h: If Your Piano Has Been in Storage
sections.body:
Pianos that have spent time in storage before reaching their new home deserve a little extra attention, and the same principles apply only more so. An instrument coming out of storage has experienced a change of environment twice over, into storage and out again, so giving it a generous settling-in period in its new room before tuning is especially important. Let it acclimatise properly to the temperature and humidity of the home before judging how it plays or booking the tuner.

If the piano was stored well, in clean, dry, stable conditions, as we always aim for when we store an instrument, it should emerge in fine shape and simply need the usual settling and tuning. When you take delivery, give the case a gentle clean to remove any dust from its time in storage, check it over for any marks, and place it thoughtfully in a good spot away from heat and damp, just as you would after any move. Then allow it those few patient weeks to find its feet.

If you are still in the middle of a move and your piano needs to go into storage before its final home, do mention it when you book, so we can wrap, store and later deliver the instrument with the care it needs and keep it in suitable conditions throughout. Looking after a piano after a move, whether it came straight across or via storage, really comes down to patience, the right spot, sensible climate and gentle everyday care, and a well-loved instrument will reward all of it for many years.

faqs: How long should I wait before playing or tuning my piano after a move?

You can play it gently fairly soon, but hold off on tuning. The piano needs a couple of weeks, often a few weeks more, to settle into the temperature and humidity of its new room. Tuning too early just means it drifts again as it acclimatises, so let it stabilise first, then book a tuner.
Where's the best place to put a piano in a new home?

Against an internal wall, away from radiators, fireplaces, external doors, draughts and direct sunlight. Heat and damp dry out or warp the wood, and sun fades the finish. A spot with a reasonably steady temperature is kindest. Tell us where you're planning to put it and we can advise during delivery.
Why does my piano sound out of tune after being moved?

It's completely normal. The travel and, above all, the change of environment nudge a finely strung instrument out of tune even when it's handled perfectly. Let it settle into the new room for a few weeks, then have it tuned, mentioning to the tuner that it has recently been moved.
Does a piano need extra care if it's been in storage?

Yes, a little. It has changed environment twice, so give it an extra-generous settling period before tuning. Clean off any dust, check it over and place it well away from heat and damp. If stored in clean, dry, stable conditions, as we aim for, it should come out in fine shape and just need the usual settling and tuning.
